speaker: interviewer
text: --6, Phyllis Dreazen, talking with Dr. Bernard Zelinger in Northbrook, Illinois on June 9, 1995. The pogrom in your city happened to people who had survived all the horrors of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: They went home. Some of them were survivors, actually, from the concentration camp. Some of them maybe survived, let's say, like I and the partisans. Some of them survived maybe where they were an Aryan papers. Some of them may be already early escapees from Russian side. You know, when the army came, and you see they, you know-- that's so-- it was a conglomeration-- survivors from the concentration camp, from the forest, from homes, from living on Aryan papers. There were about 200 some-- 12 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the boy who caused all of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They arrested him. He was in the security for a while there-- and nothing. There was-- he was in jail for a while and-- with his father. They kept him in the security building for a while-- and nothing, actually. He didn't go to jail. The people, though, the people who they found who did more the atrocities were sentenced to death. And the sentence was carried out. Because it was 1950. It was a time, you see, when the court and so forth-- it was during the regime of the-- of the socialists, or communists, whatever you call-- that, you know, there was court proceedings. And they literally took care of some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you heard about this--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you saw that certain people in the world still hated Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm proud to be a Jew. I'm proud of our tradition. I'm proud I suffer. I-- I was born as a Jew. I lived as a Jew. And I will die as a Jew, OK? The thousands of years what is carried-- I carry on the whole knowledge of the Book, which-- and-- and the hard work, and intelligence which gave the Jewish people quite a-- a lifting up of the-- of you know, of-- to be alive, and a-- and a good condition, I look at it as a benefit. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever wonder was it worthwhile to survive-- to stay in this world?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that I-- first of all, I tried to survive, because I didn't want to go like a sheep to the-- to the-- today to die. I wanted to fight. This is one. Second, this-- I think I believed in fate. I don't have the-- the-- I don't know the reason. But I think that I-- being a witness of all the atrocities, and seeing what happens now, that there are revisionists who try to deny that something like that happened, I am a witness that it really happened. And the pool of witnesses is really shrinking very rapidly. And I think that's a very good idea that somebody came out and tries to accumulate all the information from those people who were went through those tragedies and experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think it was merely fate or that you had some special talents that helped you along?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know of-- of many people who were pious, much better than me, and went-- maybe much smarter than me, maybe more active than me, and they went. It must be something else additionally to it than-- than simple-- my own energy and initiative. I think so, because the tragedy was so tremendous that-- and the indiscriminate kind of destruction, you know, I-- I-- fate pointed the finger on me, I think. And I was-- I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does that make you mad at God and anti-religious? Or does it make you think that there is such a thing as a God and make you tend to be more religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was never religious. I'm traditional, and I'm Jewish. I cannot believe that there is a God. There's something like the personification of a God, him or something like that-- it's no. And you know, scientifically, going to the evolution and so forth, it's very difficult for me to-- [LAUGHS] to understand that there is something like that that is there. And one day and one week all the world came to be like this, you see. I believe in the Big Bang story, and so forth, you know. And-- but I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you bring your children up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jew, oh yes. Yes, definitely. Because I felt, you know, what it is, you see, I felt-- like I say, I was born a Jew. I lived like a Jew. And I will die as a Jew. And I'm proud to be a Jew. And even-- I know some people who had children, didn't circumcise them, because they were afraid-- some are friends of ours. But you know, it so happens I came to the United States, which is a wonderful country, where you can express yourself very free. You can live the way you want to, within the law, and you can produce and be yourself. And this is lucky to be. Maybe that's the reason I can express myself that way. Maybe someplace else, I couldn't. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else from your family survived? And where are they now?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's only one-- from-- at this present, I have a cousin on my mother's side, who lives in Paris, where I was supposed to go there, you see? He's 81 years old. I have a seventh cousin-- second cousin, of my mother's side, who lives in Montreal-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the children of theirs. The uncle of yours that you lost--

speaker: interviewee
text: He came back. He came back to Germany again, to German occupied Poland again, like I did. He didn't go that far.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. All my entire family went-- he's not-- didn't survive. Except my one uncle went to Warsaw as a-- to fight against the Germans, and got killed in the insurrection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, medicine and healing have been very strong in your life. You've spent your life-- and you bring new life into the world. Did you, in this time, ever kill anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I did some Germans. I did some-- killed some Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, we didn't fight with the Germans. Except what happened, you see, there was a railroad going from my town to-- to Warsaw. And during the time of the war, and towards the end of the war, there were periods of time that the main railroad going through the forest by us there, the-- the railroad stopped many times. And the soldiers went out, and they had to go to-- for physiological reason. And my friend, Franek Chervitz, used to take care of them. And I helped him out. And that's all. We didn't do-- I didn't fight, actually. We did sabotage, that's all. Because we were a small group, and I didn't have no contact with a large group, because nobody wanted to take me, and nobody-- I couldn't make contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it feel like to take care of a German?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After all what I went through, and I saw German soldiers, not SS men, but really Wehrmacht, who were supposed to be-- not Hitlerites-- what they did to Jewish people, I didn't feel any bad about it to take care of them like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think it could happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everything is possible. I mean, yeah. You mean a Holocaust like this? I don't know. The Holocaust, where you take some people who didn't have to fight, OK, and to destroy them like this, you see, this is a singular kind of a Holocaust. Things happened in Burundi, it happens in-- in-- in Bosnia, and-- and-- and yeah. But over there, there's this tremendous hate of-- of the Serbians, and the Bosnian Muslims, who, during the war, they destroyed so many Serbians. They did cooperate with the-- with the Germans-- the Croats-- tremendously. I mean, they destroyed-- they were worse than the Germans-- I mean, what you read now, you see-- and the Muslims. So I-- I am for the Serbians. I'm really for the Serbians. I know. I'm not-- you know. Sure, innocent people, you know-- a-- a-- a guy, a sharpshooter, he kills somebody who runs through. That's not proper. He's not in uniform, or something like this-- civilian population, you see. But if somebody wear a uniform, who fought-- you know, when the Russian-- when the German came to Russia, right after the army was attached-- Sonderkommandos-- special commandos of destruction of the Jewish people-- right in the units. And the Wehrmacht was there. They took over a city, and they-- the people started to kill the Jewish people. They saw it. They helped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: I saw people take pictures. When we used to bury those people during the action, they came and took movies, you know, small movies, and pictures of us-- Wehrmacht, not SS. You know? Who knows where those pictures are? They some day will come out maybe 50 years from now. Who knows when. So I, at that time, I didn't feel that I'm doing something, you see, which is not proper. I didn't feel that way. I-- it's a-- it was part of the survival, and I hated German. I did hate the German at that time very much. And now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in a perspective for, you know, the new generation, you see-- the ideas like that, you see-- you have to remember. But you have to forget, too, something, you see? And you have to forgive. Time heals a lot of things, you see? And I look what Germany does now, tries to be a-- a democratic country, helped out to stand up Israel. And for example, I just read it in the paper today, or yesterday, that they're going to make one day, the 27th of January, the Holocaust Memorial Day in Germany. I mean, they try, you see. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those people who, like me, they're dying out. The new generation, I hope, will be different, you see? That's all. And they are different. They frequent Israel, they helped out, they worked, you see? I think the younger generation is different.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a wonder that a-- a society like Germany, with a Schiller and Goethe, and so forth, you see, and Schopenhauer, could do those things. I mean, it was outrageous. But-- it's possible that something like that could happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: If-- if-- if in United States, the-- the economic situation will be bad, or-- or you have to blame somebody, who knows? Who knows? There is always a possibility. It's a-- we are basically animals, you know? We are, you know. We are not-- you know, We're humans only. And the laws make it, you know, of ethics and morals, those are-- and the-- the jurisprudence, you see, and all these laws, to keep us in line. We have to-- why do we have our police? Normally, we shouldn't have any police. Why? There's police that keep us in line, and that's all. Any last thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: One last thought, you see, that's I'm-- in my-- I'm aging. And I survived. I have a nice family. I brought up two beautiful children. My son is a cardiologist-- invasive cardiologist. He made himself a tremendous, beautiful name in the hospital where I'm at, and at the school, otherwise. My daughter used to be a nurse. I direct her to be a nurse, and she was a very good nurse. She married a orthodontist. They are in love. They love each other. It's only that he had to go to-- today to Minneapolis to the meeting. And I couldn't change it, because I changed already three times the dates. So I said you cannot. So maybe there will be another occasion. And I'm satisfied. I'm serene. I-- even I wrote a little bit-- a little poem about it. That I came. You know, I said that the walk to the mountain was quite difficult, and so forth. But if I look from the top now, I'm happy and I'm serene. I'm satisfied.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a lovely note on which to end. Thank you very much for sharing--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your remarkable story with us. Thank you from the Shoah Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Thank you for giving me the ability to express my feelings and my experience, to be a witness to this, what happen. In a small way, I could do it. Thank you. And this is a picture of my mother and my sister, taken on the main street in Kielce, probably in 1938. To the left is my mother, Anna, and to the right, with the fur coat or collar, is my sister, Lucy.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- my sister, Lucy, who works in the ghetto hospitals. She probably picks up some clothes for the patients at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken approximately in 1942-- about maybe the first few months of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did she wind up? Where did she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Treblinka. She went to Treblinka. This is my Uncle Solomon, my father's brother, who was the chairman of an organization of the area of Kielce in [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a revisionist kind of a organization-- Zionist. [NON-ENGLISH] Zionist. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a-- a group picture of our first grade, probably, from 1926, with the teacher by the name of Guta Kaufman. And I was her pet student. I'm sitting to the left of her legs-- on the floor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture from Spas, S-P-A-S, a military ROTC group, which every student in the lyceum, in the junior college, had to attend. We used to go in summer or in winter for six weeks of military preparation. I am there on the second from the left side, kneeling down with a gun in my hand. This is a picture of our family during the wedding. There is my father-in-law, Mr. Rinske. Mrs. Rinske is sitting down; Helen's brother, Henry, standing; I, near by him, and my wife already at this time, sitting down.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Wasser, who I escaped with from the Henrykow camp, and stayed with him in the partisans. He's not anymore alive. He died about eight years ago, and he lived in Scranton, Pennsylvania. His wife is alive, and I'm in close contact with her. This is the extended family of the German people who I lived with after coming to Germany and studying at the medical school. Sitting down from the left side is Mr. [NON-ENGLISH] sister, Elizabeth. Sitting by him on the left is Ann [NON-ENGLISH] his wife, and he's sitting near to the right of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Stanislav Kosubec, who at the time, when I was in the partisan, was about 16-years-old. And this is Mrs. Kosubec, Joseph Kosubec's wife, who was a partisan. This is a picture of all the interns in 1950 at Mt. Sinai Hospital in Milwaukee.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the first from the right side. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I'm surrounded here by my entire family-- my wife, Helen, to my left; my daughter, Lucy, to my right; her husband, Richard, to her right. To the left of my wife is my son, Allan, then, Marissa, his daughter, and Karen, his wife. Kneeling down, or sitting downstairs, is Lauren to the left, and the middle is Bradley, my grandson, and Ariel on the right side.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK. Do you want to say anything about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- yeah. I just finished recording my life story from early years to the time of my arrival to this beautiful country of ours. The emphasis of my statement is on the years of the Holocaust and the history of our suffering during the era of Nazism. I hope that this statement will help to deny those people who negate the factual history of the Holocaust. The pool of witnesses is shrinking rapidly. It is my fervent hope that our Jewish nation's suffering will never happen again. I was born as a Jew. I lived as a Jew, and was persecuted only because I was a Jew. I am proud of my heritage and the thousand years of history of the Jewish people. Our accomplishments are based on the product of our faith, knowledge of the Book, and hard work. Our people's motto is justice and personal liberty, with freedom and equality for all. I know that you, my children, grandchildren, will carry the same qualities as you, as citizens of the great country of ours. Never forget. God bless you. Love.

speaker: interviewee
text: What did you say that for?

speaker: interviewer
text: Allan, do you want to add anything from the previous statement about maybe being a child of a survivor, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd like to, first of all, really express a certain gratitude. I would say, based on the opportunity of--

speaker: interviewee
text: --of having the ability to capture some of my family's past history, a history that really was lost to some degree because of the great silence that surrounds the personal stories of the Holocaust. Every-- every one of us here knows that there was the Holocaust, and-- and the overall impact of that event. But many of our--

speaker: interviewee
text: --personal histories have been silent-- almost secret-- and not ever discussed in great detail. And I have not yet heard this history, which-- which occurred today, but I look forward to it, because it's something that I didn't have an opportunity to connect with. And I think that having that will-- will bring us closer together as a family, and also give me something that I never had as a child-- fully, which is the true story of-- of my origins, and our past as a family. So I'm-- I'm glad for this-- this event and this undertaking. Thanks again for helping us perform this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lucy, anything that you'd like to add? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think being the child that-- of a survivor, or survivors, is-- is a unique, and-- and special, and sometimes difficult position to grow up with. [LAUGHS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I-- I too, am happy for sharing this, and having it come out. I think both of my parents are true survivors. And I-- I'm happy that we're all here, and we're also celebrating our Jewishness, and continuing the traditions, which I know from my parents is a very important issue. [LAUGHS] Don't cry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just wanted to say, I love guys. [CRYING] I can't help it. You know, he's-- he's celebrated life. And he has his family around him. He brought thousands of babies into the world. And I think he is a true survivor. He encountered death so many times, and pushed and pushed. And I think his perseverance is a good lesson for all of us. And I know that I have that in my heart. And I'm sorry I'm crying. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. I can't help it. I'm silly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else want to say anything? Go ahead. Ariel.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that my papa is very nice, and I love him a lot. And he's taught me a lot about being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone else want to share? Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of being born in this country to two Jewish parents, and growing up in a-- a I would consider it, a very Jewish household. I think my life has been changed considerably being part of this family-- in particular, in a relationship to-- being part of a family of-- of survivors. And I think that what-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] --I think what mom and dad should know that-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- I'm changed about how I feel about my Jewishness because of it. And I know that I'll never forget. And I know that the lessons that we'll teach your grandchildren will be that they will never forget. And I know that they will teach their children never to forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mommy-- went in a-- I don't know. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Bradley, it's OK, honey. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ariel? Bradley. We don't have to talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm just going to say, bye bye!

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to-- oh. Lauren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I want to thank him again, because he came into my class when we were studying immigrants and talked about his life in front of my class. And I want to thank him again for coming in, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to just say that--

speaker: interviewee
text: --these-- the stories are being recorded and will be available for the whole world to-- to learn and be educated, and to hear the real true stories of--

speaker: interviewee
text: --of you know, what had happened. And it's great for us as a family to learn that as well, and everybody. So I think it's a very important thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you all very much, on behalf of the Shoah Foundation. And thank you, Dr. and Mrs. Zelinger.
